title.emphasis: Part III: Synthesis and Analysis: Designing the Tradable Political Cooperative Security

section.para: Building upon the deconstructed legal and financial frameworks, this part synthesizes these components into concrete, actionable models. It begins by examining existing innovative cooperative structures that serve as precedents, then applies the critical Howey test to determine the security status of the proposed instruments, and culminates in the detailed design of three distinct models for a political cooperative.

title.emphasis: Section 6: Precedents and Pathfinders: New Generation and Multi-Stakeholder Models

section.para: The concept of a tradable cooperative security, while novel, does not emerge from a vacuum. For decades, cooperatives have experimented with their structures to address the inherent tension between democratic principles and the need for capital. Examining these evolutionary steps provides a solid foundation and valuable precedents for the more radical models proposed in this report.

title.emphasis: New Generation Cooperatives (NGCs)

section.para: Pioneered in the upper Midwest in the 1970s, the New Generation Cooperative (NGC) represents a significant departure from traditional cooperative structure, particularly in agricultural processing. NGCs are designed to handle the large, upfront capital requirements of value-added processing facilities, a challenge traditional models struggle to meet. Their key innovations are:

listitem.para: Tradable Delivery Rights: The cornerstone of the NGC is the creation of an equity instrument known as a "delivery right" or "delivery share". This is not merely a membership certificate; it is a tradable and appreciable asset that represents both the right and the obligation to deliver a specific quantity and quality of a commodity to the cooperative's processing facility. The value of these rights can fluctuate based on the cooperative's profitability and market conditions, making them analogous to shares of corporate stock. This establishes a crucial precedent: a cooperative instrument that is designed to appreciate and be traded, albeit often in a limited market with board approval required for transfers.

Closed Membership and Upfront Equity: Unlike traditional open-membership co-ops, NGCs have a closed or restricted membership. Membership is contingent upon purchasing a substantial number of delivery rights, which provides the cooperative with a significant upfront equity base to fund its capital-intensive projects. This structure directly links ownership and benefit not just to patronage, but to a significant initial capital investment, blurring the lines of traditional cooperative finance.59

title.emphasis: Multi-Stakeholder and Hybrid Cooperatives

section.para: Another important evolutionary path is the development of multi-stakeholder cooperatives. These organizations formally include more than one class of stakeholder in their governance and economic structure. A common example is a food co-op that includes consumer-members, worker-members, and perhaps even producer-members or community supporters.

This model is significant because it provides a governance precedent for managing the potentially conflicting interests of different groups within a single cooperative entity. The bylaws of a multi-stakeholder co-op must explicitly define the rights, responsibilities, and economic participation of each class, including how board seats are allocated and how profits are distributed among the different groups.60 This demonstrates that a cooperative structure is flexible enough to accommodate heterogeneous member classes with divergent interests, a necessary feature for any model that seeks to integrate passive capital investors alongside active member-patrons.

title.emphasis: California's "Community Investor" Model

section.para: A particularly relevant legal innovation is the California Worker Cooperative Act (formerly AB 816), which amended the state's Consumer Cooperative Corporation Law to create a specific legal framework for worker cooperatives. A key provision of this law was the creation of a new class of participant: the "community investor".

A community investor is defined as a person who is not a worker-member but who invests money in the cooperative. This law provides a direct statutory template for integrating outside capital while preserving democratic control:

listitem.para: Limited Voting Rights: Community investors can be granted limited voting rights, but these rights are explicitly restricted. Their voting power can only extend to approval rights on major existential decisions, such as a merger, sale of major assets, or dissolution. They have no right to elect directors or propose actions.

Securities Exemption: The law also raised the exemption from securities registration for cooperative memberships from $300 to $1,000 per member, making it easier for co-ops to raise community capital. Subsequent legislation (AB 283) further clarified that equity-related patronage distributions are also exempt from qualification requirements, removing a significant administrative burden.

section.para: The California model is a landmark. It provides a clear, state-sanctioned legal pathway for a cooperative to issue a separate class of shares to non-patron investors, grant them a financial return, and strictly limit their governance rights to protect the democratic control of the worker-members. This is a direct and powerful precedent for the "Dual-Class Influence Share" model proposed later in this report.

title.emphasis: Table 1: Comparative Analysis of Cooperative Models

section.para: To visualize the evolution of cooperative structures, the following table compares key features across different models, illustrating the lineage from traditional forms to the innovative structures that inform the political cooperative concept.

row.entry: Capital Investment (purchase of delivery rights) & Patronage
Patronage by different stakeholder classes (e.g., consumers, workers)
Democratic control, but board representation may be allocated by stakeholder class
One Worker-Member, One Vote; Community Investors have limited/no voting rights
Minimal member buy-in; retained patronage earnings
Significant upfront equity from sale of delivery rights; retained earnings
Member buy-in from multiple classes; retained earnings
Worker-member buy-in; sale of non-voting shares to community investors
Non-tradable, non-appreciable, redeemable at par value
Tradable (limited market), appreciable, tied to delivery obligation
Varies by class; typically non-tradable for patronage classes
Non-tradable for worker-members; potentially tradable for community investors
Member-patrons, through services at cost and patronage refunds
Member-patrons, through market access and share of processing profits
Multiple stakeholder classes, according to bylaws
Worker-members (via patronage) and Community Investors (via financial return)

section.para: This comparative analysis demonstrates a clear trend: cooperative models have evolved to become more sophisticated in their approach to capital. They have developed mechanisms to require more substantial upfront investment (NGCs) and to formally incorporate the interests of different classes of participants (Multi-Stakeholder and California models). These precedents show that the cooperative structure is not static; it is an adaptable framework that can be modified to meet complex capital and governance challenges, paving the way for the political cooperative.

title.emphasis: Section 7: The Howey Test Gauntlet: Is the Political Cooperative Share a Security?

section.para: Any attempt to create a tradable financial instrument in the United States must confront the nation's securities laws, which are designed to protect investors through mandatory disclosure and registration. The foundational legal standard for determining whether a particular financial arrangement constitutes a "security" is the Howey test, established by the Supreme Court in the 1946 case SEC v. W.J. Howey Co.. A rigorous analysis of how the proposed political cooperative securities would be treated under this test is not merely a compliance exercise; it is fundamental to their design.

title.emphasis: The Howey Test Defined

section.para: The Howey test provides four prongs to identify an "investment contract," which is a type of security. A transaction is an investment contract if it involves:

listitem.para: An investment of money (or other valuable assets);
In a common enterprise;
With a reasonable expectation of profits;
To be derived from the entrepreneurial or managerial efforts of others.

section.para: The test is designed to be flexible, focusing on the economic reality and substance of a transaction rather than its form or name. If an instrument meets these four criteria, it is a security and is subject to regulation by the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

title.emphasis: Application to Cooperative Interests

section.para: Historically, the application of the Howey test to cooperatives has been fact-dependent and has evolved with the complexity of cooperative models.

listitem.para: Traditional Cooperatives: The equity interests in traditional cooperatives are generally not considered securities. In the landmark case United Housing Foundation, Inc. v. Forman, the Supreme Court analyzed shares in a housing cooperative and found they were not securities. The Court reasoned that there was no "investment" with an "expectation of profits"; rather, the residents were purchasing a place to live.62 The economic benefits were derived from the members' own use and participation, not "solely from the efforts of others".62 The SEC has consistently issued guidance that common shares in a cooperative are not securities, provided they do not pay dividends, are not freely transferable, and grant only one vote per member, thus lacking the typical features of an investment.63

New Generation Cooperatives (NGCs): The analysis for NGCs is far more complex and points toward their equity interests being classified as securities. The substantial upfront capital investment clearly satisfies the "investment of money" prong. The cooperative itself is a "common enterprise." The tradable and appreciable nature of the delivery rights creates a clear "expectation of profits" through capital gains, which is distinct from patronage refunds. Finally, while the member must deliver a commodity, the ultimate profitability of the enterprise depends heavily on the "essential managerial efforts" of the cooperative's management in processing and marketing the final product, satisfying the "efforts of others" prong.62 For these reasons, many legal analyses conclude that NGC delivery rights are likely securities, and cooperatives are often advised to operate under this assumption, typically by seeking exemptions from full registration.

title.emphasis: Analysis of Proposed Political Cooperative Securities

section.para: Any of the proposed models for a political cooperative that involve issuing a tradable instrument to passive investors in exchange for capital with the promise of a financial return will almost certainly be classified as a security under the Howey test. The entire purpose of such an instrument is to function as an investment.
A prong-by-prong analysis confirms this conclusion:

listitem.para: Investment of Money: Investors would purchase "Influence Shares" or asset-backed securities with cash or other assets. This prong is clearly met.
Common Enterprise: Investors would be pooling their capital in a common venture—the political cooperative or its associated SPV—whose fortunes are tied together. This prong is clearly met.62

Expectation of Profits: The instrument would be marketed to investors with the explicit or implicit promise of financial returns, either through dividends from non-patronage income, interest payments from an asset-backed security, or capital appreciation upon resale in a secondary market. This is the very definition of profit from an investment perspective and is distinct from the patronage-based savings of traditional members.62

From the Efforts of Others: The investors in these models are passive capital providers. Their financial return depends entirely on the "essential managerial efforts" of the cooperative's leadership and staff to successfully execute its political strategy and generate non-patronage income. The investors themselves perform no labor for the cooperative.

section.para: Given that all four prongs are met, the conclusion is inescapable: the proposed instruments are investment contracts and therefore securities. This is not a barrier but a critical design parameter. It means the cooperative must embrace this status and structure its capital-raising activities in full compliance with federal and state securities laws, either through full registration or, more likely, by utilizing an available exemption.

title.emphasis: Table 2: Application of the
Test to a Proposed Cooperative Security (Model B: Dual-Class)

section.para: This table provides a structured legal analysis of the proposed dual-class cooperative security, outlining the likely outcome under each prong of the Howey test and suggesting a corresponding compliance strategy.

row.entry: Class I shares are purchased by external investors with cash, representing a clear investment of money.
*SEC v. Howey Co.* established that an investment of money is the first element. Digital asset frameworks confirm this applies to any exchange of value.
Acknowledge this prong is met. Structure the offering documents to clearly define the investment terms.
Investors' funds are pooled within the cooperative, and their fortunes are interwoven with the success of the cooperative's non-patronage activities. This constitutes a horizontal commonality.
A cooperative is clearly a "common enterprise".62 Courts have readily found this prong met in various collective investment schemes.
Acknowledge this prong is met. The cooperative's articles and bylaws will define the common enterprise.

Class I shares are designed to provide a financial return through appreciation and/or dividends from non-patronage income. This is distinct from a member's patronage refund, which is a rebate.62 The potential for resale on a secondary market reinforces this profit motive.
*Forman* distinguished co-op membership (no profit motive) from investments. NGCs, with their appreciable rights, demonstrate that cooperative instruments *can* have a profit motive.62
Acknowledge this prong is met. Clearly disclose in offering materials that returns are derived from non-patronage sources and are not guaranteed, distinguishing them from patronage refunds.

Class I investors are passive. Their financial return is entirely dependent on the managerial, strategic, and operational efforts of the cooperative's board and staff to generate non-patronage income through political activities.
The "essential managerial efforts" of the cooperative's managers, not the investor's own labor, determine the success of the enterprise, satisfying this prong.
Acknowledge this prong is met. The governance structure, which explicitly limits investor involvement (e.g., non-voting shares), reinforces their passive role.
The "Influence Share" is an investment contract and therefore a **security**.
The economic reality of the transaction is that of a passive investment for profit.

Do not attempt to structure the instrument to fail the test. Instead, **embrace its status as a security** and plan for compliance with securities law, likely through an exemption from full registration (e.g., Regulation D, Regulation Crowdfunding, or state-level exemptions).

section.para: This analysis provides a clear compliance roadmap. Instead of trying to contort the instrument to avoid being a security—which would defeat its purpose—the political cooperative should be designed from the outset to comply with securities regulations, ensuring legal durability and investor protection.

title.emphasis: Section 8: Proposed Models for a Political Cooperative

section.para: Synthesizing the legal, financial, and political frameworks analyzed thus far, it is possible to construct specific, actionable models for a political cooperative. Each model offers a different approach to balancing democratic control, capital formation, and political efficacy. The following three models represent a spectrum from a more conventional, structured-finance approach to a radically decentralized, market-driven one.

title.emphasis: Model A: The Securitized Political Action Fund

section.para: This model leverages the established architecture of securitization to create a clean separation between the cooperative's core functions and its investment vehicle. It prioritizes legal and financial insulation over structural simplicity.

listitem.para: Cooperative Formation: A cooperative is formed for a primary purpose (e.g., a consumer co-op for its members, a worker co-op for its employees). This establishes the patronage-based side of the enterprise.

Non-Patronage Business Line: The cooperative's board, exercising its business judgment, establishes political action as a distinct, non-patronage business activity. This could involve creating an in-house division or a wholly-owned subsidiary (a standard C-corp or LLC) dedicated to political consulting, lobbying, or managing independent expenditure campaigns. Under the "actually facilitates" test, this activity would be structured to "merely enhance overall profitability" rather than being "integrally intertwined" with the co-op's primary patronage function.24

True Sale to SPV: The cooperative (the Originator) enters into a contract to sell the rights to the future net income stream from this political action subsidiary to a bankruptcy-remote Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV). This "true sale" legally transfers ownership of the future profits to the SPV in exchange for an upfront cash payment.

Securities Issuance: The SPV, now owning the income stream, issues tradable, asset-backed securities to the public or to accredited investors. These securities can be tranched into senior, mezzanine, and equity layers to appeal to different investor risk profiles. The payments of principal and interest to investors are funded exclusively by the cash flows generated by the political action subsidiary and collected by the SPV.

Governance: Governance is bifurcated. The member-patrons retain absolute one-member, one-vote control over the cooperative and its primary business. The investors who purchase the asset-backed securities have no membership, no voting rights, and no legal claim against the cooperative itself. Their legal relationship is solely with the SPV, and their claim is limited to the assets held by the SPV.

Pros: This model offers the strongest legal and financial separation between the democratic cooperative and the capital markets. It utilizes a well-understood and legally robust framework (securitization) that is designed precisely for this kind of asset isolation.

Cons: The structure is complex and carries significant transactional costs to set up. Its success hinges on the ability of the political action arm to generate a sufficiently predictable and substantial profit stream to make the securities attractive to investors, a significant challenge given the inherent uncertainty of political outcomes. Furthermore, the non-patronage income would be subject to corporate income tax at the cooperative/subsidiary level before being transferred to the SPV.

title.emphasis: Model B: The Dual-Class "Influence Share" Cooperative

section.para: This model is structurally simpler and draws directly from precedents like the California "Community Investor" law. It integrates the investment mechanism directly into the cooperative's capital structure.

listitem.para: Cooperative Incorporation: A cooperative corporation is formed under a state statute that explicitly allows for multiple classes of membership or shares, such as California's Cooperative Corporation Law.
Issuance of Two Share Classes: The cooperative's articles and bylaws authorize two distinct classes of shares:

Class M (Member Shares): These are the patronage shares. One share is issued per member, entitling the holder to one vote in all cooperative matters and to receive patronage dividends derived from the cooperative's primary business activities. These shares are non-tradable and redeemable only at their par value.15

Class I (Influence Shares): These are the investment shares. They are sold to the public (members or non-members) and are designed to be tradable on a secondary market. They carry no voting rights, or, following the California model, strictly limited approval rights on existential matters like dissolution or merger. Holders of Class I shares are entitled to receive dividends paid exclusively from the cooperative's after-tax non-patronage income.

Governance: Democratic control is fully preserved within the Class M member-shareholders, who are the only ones with the power to elect the board and direct the cooperative's affairs.15 Class I investors are passive capital providers, legally firewalled from governance by the share structure.

Pros: This model is structurally simpler and more direct than securitization. It creates a tradable instrument that is part of the cooperative itself, potentially fostering a greater sense of alignment between investors and the organization's mission.

Cons: It creates a direct potential for agency conflict. The board, elected by Class M members, must manage the cooperative for their benefit while also generating non-patronage profits to satisfy Class I investors. This could lead to difficult decisions where the interests of the two groups diverge. This model also requires careful navigation of state-level cooperative laws and federal securities regulations.63

title.emphasis: Model C: The Futarchy-Governed Political Fund

section.para: This model represents the most radical departure from traditional structures, embracing decentralized technology and prediction markets to formalize a market for effective political decision-making.

listitem.para: Entity Formation: The organization is likely formed as a Decentralized Autonomous Organization (DAO), a digitally native entity governed by smart contracts on a blockchain, though it would still need a legal wrapper (like an LLC or unincorporated association) to interact with the traditional legal and financial world.
Governance by Futarchy: The core of the model is futarchy, a governance system where participants "vote on values, but bet on beliefs".65

Voting on Values: The members of the DAO (who could join via a token purchase) vote democratically on a clear, measurable, high-level goal. For example: "The objective for this fiscal year is to pass legislation that reduces carbon emissions in State X by 10%," or "The objective is to ensure Candidate Y wins the election."

Betting on Beliefs: Once the goal is set, specific proposals for action are put forth (e.g., "Fund a $1M ad campaign targeting these districts," "Lobby these specific legislators," "Fund a grassroots organizing effort"). For each proposal, a prediction market is created.66 Investors (who can be members or the general public) buy and sell tokens that represent the predicted success of that proposal in achieving the democratically chosen goal. The price of the "Pass" token for a given proposal reflects the market's collective belief in its efficacy.

Automated Execution: The proposal whose prediction market token reaches the highest price (or passes a certain threshold) is automatically funded and executed by the DAO's smart contracts.65 Investors are rewarded financially based on the accuracy of their predictions, not on the political outcome itself.

Governance: This model redefines democratic control. The "demos" (members) sets the ultimate direction and defines success, while the "market" (investors) determines the most efficient strategy to get there. It directly incentivizes participants to make rational, informed decisions rather than emotional or biased ones, as they have "skin in the game".67

Pros: This is the purest formalization of a market for effective political influence. It has the potential to be radically more efficient and effective at allocating political capital than traditional top-down decision-making. It aligns financial incentives directly with achieving stated goals.

Cons: The model is highly experimental and technologically intensive. It faces enormous legal and regulatory uncertainty, as DAOs and futarchy exist in a legal gray area. The greatest challenge is defining and objectively measuring the "success" of a political goal, which is often far more complex than a simple token price metric used in other DAO futarchy experiments.66

title.emphasis: Section 9: Governance, Agency Costs, and Compliance

section.para: The viability of any political cooperative model, particularly the hybrid structures of Models A and B, hinges on its ability to manage the inherent conflicts of interest between its constituent groups and to navigate a labyrinth of regulatory requirements. This section analyzes these governance challenges through the lens of mechanism design and outlines the necessary compliance architecture.

title.emphasis: Agency Problems and Mechanism Design

section.para: In corporate governance, an "agency problem" arises when the interests of an "agent" (e.g., a company's management) diverge from those of the "principal" (e.g., the shareholders).68 The political cooperative creates a complex, multi-layered agency problem:

listitem.para: Vertical Conflict: The classic conflict between owners (the cooperative as a whole) and management (the board and staff) exists, where managers might pursue their own interests.68

Horizontal Conflict: A new and more acute conflict arises between the two classes of principals: the member-patrons (Class M) and the capital investors (Class I). Member-patrons may prioritize long-term community benefits, lower-cost services, or ideological purity, while capital investors will exclusively prioritize the maximization of financial returns from the non-patronage political activities.71

section.para: Mechanism design theory, a field of economics that studies how to design institutions to achieve desired outcomes given actors' self-interest, provides a framework for addressing these conflicts.73 The goal is to design a set of rules—a mechanism—that aligns the incentives of all parties. For the political cooperative, this would involve:

listitem.para: Governance Structure: The dual-class share structure (Model B) or the SPV firewall (Model A) are themselves forms of mechanism design, intended to legally separate the control rights of members from the financial rights of investors. The bylaws must be meticulously drafted to reinforce this separation, for example, by establishing a super-majority vote of members to alter the profit-split formula between patronage and non-patronage activities, preventing it from becoming a recurring political issue.61

Executive Compensation: The compensation for the cooperative's management could be structured to align incentives. For example, bonuses could be tied to a balanced scorecard that measures both the delivery of benefits to member-patrons and the profitability of the non-patronage business line, forcing management to serve both masters.

Information Disclosure: Transparency is a key tool for mitigating agency costs. The cooperative would need to provide clear and separate financial reporting for its patronage and non-patronage activities, allowing both members and investors to monitor performance and hold management accountable.

title.emphasis: Compliance Architecture

section.para: Operating a political cooperative would require simultaneous compliance with at least four distinct and demanding regulatory regimes. A failure in any one area could jeopardize the entire enterprise.

listitem.para: Entity Formation and Cooperative Law: The organization must be properly incorporated, likely as a cooperative corporation or an LLC electing to be taxed as a C-Corporation, to access Subchapter T benefits. The choice of state for incorporation is critical; a state like California with explicit statutes for multi-stakeholder or community-investor models would be highly advantageous. The articles of incorporation and bylaws must be carefully drafted to define the rights of each class of shareholder and to establish the pre-existing obligation to pay patronage dividends, a requirement for Subchapter T.

Federal and State Campaign Finance Law: As an entity engaged in political spending, the cooperative and/or its associated vehicles would fall under the jurisdiction of the Federal Election Commission (FEC) and state election boards.

Registration: If the cooperative establishes a traditional PAC (SSF), it must register with the FEC.44 If it creates a Super PAC for its independent expenditures, that entity must also register.74 A 501(c)(4) must register with the IRS.47

Disclosure: All political spending—contributions from a PAC or independent expenditures from the cooperative's treasury or a Super PAC—would be subject to strict and periodic public disclosure requirements, detailing the amounts and recipients of the spending.58

Coordination Rules: The cooperative must implement a rigorous compliance program to ensure that any independent expenditures are not coordinated with candidate campaigns, as coordination would transform the spending into an illegal corporate contribution.58
IRS Tax Law (Subchapter T): The cooperative must meticulously track and separate its income into patronage and non-patronage sources to comply with Subchapter T.
Form 1120-C: The cooperative would file its annual income tax return using Form 1120-C, U.S. Income Tax Return for Cooperative Associations.22

Form 1099-PATR: For every member receiving over $10 in patronage dividends, the cooperative must file Form 1099-PATR, Taxable Distributions Received From Cooperatives.22 This form details the amount of the dividend, distinguishing between cash and non-cash (equity) portions.
SEC Securities Law: As established by the Howey test analysis, the tradable "Influence Shares" would be considered securities. This triggers the jurisdiction of the SEC.

Registration or Exemption: The offering of these shares must either be registered with the SEC—a costly and complex process—or qualify for an exemption. Common exemptions include Regulation D (for offerings to accredited investors), Regulation A+ (a "mini-IPO"), or Regulation Crowdfunding (for raising smaller amounts from the general public).

State "Blue Sky" Laws: In addition to federal law, the cooperative must comply with the securities laws (known as "blue sky" laws) of every state in which it offers shares. While most states have exemptions for cooperatives, these vary and must be carefully navigated.63

Anti-Fraud Provisions: Regardless of registration status, all securities offerings are subject to the anti-fraud provisions of the securities acts, which prohibit any material misstatements or omissions in connection with the sale of a security.

title.emphasis: Table 3: Political Financing Vehicle Comparison

section.para: This table situates the proposed political cooperative (Model B) within the existing landscape of political financing entities, highlighting its unique combination of features.

row.entry: State Cooperative Corp. Law; IRC Subchapter T
Individuals from a restricted class (e.g., employees, members)
No limit on independent expenditures; limited contributions to candidates
Unlimited independent expenditures; no contributions to candidates
Unlimited lobbying; political activity cannot be primary purpose
Unlimited independent expenditures; no direct contributions to candidates
Strictly prohibited from coordinating with candidates
Prohibited from coordinating expenditures with federal candidates
Strictly prohibited from coordinating with candidates
N/A (Source is revenue); Shareholder identity may be public or private depending on market

section.para: This comparison clarifies the unique position of the political cooperative. Unlike all other vehicles, its political spending is not funded by contributions but by operational revenue generated from its non-patronage business line and capitalized by the sale of securities. This creates a self-sustaining political-financial engine, a structure fundamentally different from anything in the current landscape.
